covered_objectives: LO1 — Define confirmation bias in plain language and identify it as a systematic (not random) tendency of human cognition. [q1]
LO2 — Describe the three mechanisms through which confirmation bias operates: selective search, biased interpretation, and selective memory. [q2]
LO3 — Explain why confirmation bias is automatic and effortless by connecting it to System 1 heuristic processing and cognitive economy. [q3]
LO4 — Recall Peter Wason's 2-4-6 task as experimental evidence that people seek confirming rather than disconfirming evidence. [q4]
LO5 — Apply the concept of confirmation bias to the context of evaluating AI outputs, identifying how the bias can cause a practitioner to misjudge an AI system's quality. [q5]
LO6 — Recognise that awareness of a bias does not automatically eliminate it, and that motivated reasoning can persist even under deliberate System 2 thinking. [q6]
objective_coverage.notes: All 6 LOs have exactly one mapped question each. objective_ref values in sidecar match corpus §3 LOs at high similarity. No orphan questions. Pass.

beginner_scope.notes: All stems use plain language. No unexplained acronyms. 'System 1' and 'System 2' references are valid — both terms defined in prerequisite topics 9.1 and 9.2 listed in corpus §2. 'Motivated reasoning' and 'cognitive economy' are defined within this topic's corpus. No forward references to topics 9.4+. No out_of_scope_question findings. No jargon_in_stem findings. Distractors draw exclusively on beginner misconceptions about confirmation bias. Pass.

verdict: Pass — all five checks clean; q6 correct answer is notably longer than its distractors (flagged for author awareness) but longest_correct_rate of 0.167 is well under the 0.40 hard-fail threshold.
